_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The Shiller CAPE (Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings) ratio divides the current price of the S&P 500 by the average inflation-adjusted earnings over the past ten years, smoothing earnings volatility. Currently at 42.18 (July 2026), it vastly exceeds its 140-year average of 17.3 and approaches the all-time peak of 44.19 reached in December 1999 during the dot-com bubble. Elevated CAPE readings historically signal overvaluation, with a base rate indicating that when CAPE is above ~39, subsequent 1- to 2-year returns tend to be negative, averaging -4% over one year and -20% over two years. However, while CAPE conveys valuation extremes and magnitude of correction risks, it does not specify timing, as market liquidity conditions can sustain valuations longer than fundamentals suggest.
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator measures total US equity market capitalization divided by nominal GDP, reflecting how equity prices relate to the overall economic output. At 231.7% (June 2026), this reading greatly surpasses prior cycle peaks of 146% at the dot-com peak and 110% in 2007, underscoring a historically stretched market relative to the economy. Warren Buffett has described excesses above 200% as 'playing with fire,' indicating increased vulnerability as returns become harder to justify economically and the risk of price corrections rises with market dislocations. This metric's limitation is that GDP may not perfectly capture economic productivity changes or global market exposures, so while it flags systemic valuation strain, it cannot time a market turn on its own.
notes.concentration: Market concentration reflects the proportion of the S&P 500 capitalization held by its largest constituents. Currently, the top 5 stocks comprise approximately 29.8% of the index, the tech sector weighs 48%, and the top 10 hold 41.0%, a sharp increase from 17.5% in 2015. Elevated concentration can amplify systemic risk as market moves by a few large-cap growth names disproportionately influence overall performance, increasing vulnerability to sentiment shifts or liquidity drying up in these key sectors. However, structural changes in market composition and investor behavior mean high concentration signals fragility but does not precisely time corrections, as momentum and liquidity factors interplay dynamically.
notes.yieldcurve: The yield curve spread between 10-year Treasury yields and 3-month Treasury bills, currently 0.71 percentage points (July 2026), serves as a barometer of economic expectations and monetary policy stance. A positive but relatively low spread corresponds with a 31% recession probability per the NY Fed model, indicating heightened economic uncertainty but not an imminent downturn. Flattened or inverted curves historically precede recessions by tightening financial conditions and signaling slowing growth, which can trigger liquidity contractions ending bull markets. Nonetheless, yield curve signals are probabilistic and timing can be variable, so while they help anticipate risk windows, they do not precisely dictate when market liquidity – the 'music' – will cease.
notes.drypowder: Dry powder refers to cash reserves held by investors available for deploying into assets; Berkshire Hathaway’s record $397.4 billion cash hoard (Q1 2026) exemplifies this. High dry powder implies latent buying power that can support markets, but with 79% of institutions expecting a cash decline, this suggests anticipated liquidity drawdowns or capital deployment imminently. Liquidity availability underpins market resilience; when dry powder contracts or investors shift to cash, it can precipitate rapid market moves as fewer funds chase assets. A caveat is that dry powder levels reflect current positioning but can shift quickly with macro signals, so they identify potential liquidity shocks but do not predict exact timing.
notes.finale: Valuation measures like the Shiller CAPE and Buffett Indicator quantify the potential magnitude of market corrections by highlighting extremes relative to historical norms; these high readings indicate that the 'stakes' in this game of musical chairs are very large. However, the timing of when the market downturn begins depends on a liquidity shock, akin to the moment the music stops. The imminent IPO lock-up expirations from December 2026 to May 2027, involving major issuers such as SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic, represent a plausible trigger window as large insider share sales increase supply and test market demand. This confluence of stretched valuations and a foreseeable liquidity event underscores the need to distinguish between valuation-driven risk magnitude and trigger-driven timing in understanding market vulnerabilities.
